coredata.dc:title: Enhanced-fluorescent imaging and targeted therapy of liver cancer using highly luminescent carbon dots-conjugated foliate
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shicai
Chen, Lin
Wang, Junli
Du, Jinglei
Li, Qiang
Gao, Yuduan
Yu, Shiping
Yang, Yongzhen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown great potential in drug delivery and biological imaging applications. In this work, a doxorubicin (DOX) delivery carrier and imaging probe for liver cancer-targeted therapy was designed based on CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (97%), aiming to enhance the antitumor activity and imaging efficiency. Folic acid (FA), which showed high expression in hepatoma cells, was used as targeting components to modify CDs (FA-CDs), and then FA-CDs-DOX was obtained by loading DOX. Results show that CDs and FA-CDs have good biocompatibility, and the DOX release from FA-CDs-DOX is targeted and selective. Confocal microscope demonstrates that FA-CDs-DOX has excellent ability of fluorescence imaging in liver cancer cells. The imaging in vivo shows the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs-DOX is strong enough to penetrate tumor tissue and skin, further verifying its enhanced-fluorescent imaging effects. Tumor inhibition in vivo indicates that the targeting ability of FA-CDs-DOX is significantly higher than that of free DOX, showing obvious better therapeutic effect. To sum up, the targeted and fluorescent drug delivery system based on CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield show an excellent imaging in vivo and tumor inhibition effect, which provide a novel strategy for promoting the potential clinical application of CDs in liver cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly luminescent carbon dots
Enhanced-fluorescent imaging
Targeted therapy
Liver cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of FA-CDs-DOX by loading DOX
Targeting imaging in vitro and in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM image of CDs (Inset image is the photograph of CDs solution excited with 365 nm UV light), (c) zeta potential of different products' PBS solution.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of different products (a) and (b); the standard curves of CDs (c) and DOX (d).
Drug release of CDs-DOX at different pH values.
Intracellular release of CDs-DOX in HepG2 cells: 0.5 h (a–d), 4 h (e–h) and 8 h (i–l).

Intracellular release of CDs-DOX: intracellular release of DOX from CDs-DOX (a) and free DOX (b) in the HepG2 cells; (c) intracellular release of DOX from CDs-DOX in 7702 cells; (d) intensity comparison of the release amount of DOX.
Viability of HepG2, HeLa, 7702 and 293 T cells after incubating with CDs for 24 h (a) and 48 h (b).
Apoptosis of 7702 cells for the blank control group (a), incubated with CDs for 12 h (b) and 24 h (c).
CLSM images of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs and FA-CDs under excitation wavelengths of 405 nm for 0.5 h, separately.
Fluorescence imaging of FA-CDs-DOX in HepG2 cells for 24 h.
Photographs of the CDs, DOX and FA-CDs-DOX suspension excited by 480 nm light (a) and their fluorescence imaging for mice (b).
DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX were incubated with HepG2 cells for 24 (a) and 48 h (b) and with 7702 cells for 24 (c) and 48 h (d).
The change of tumor volume for each group with treatment time (a), the photograph of tumor treated by different groups (b and c) and photos of mice with tumor treated by different groups.
Organ imaging after treatment with free DOX and FA-CDs -DOX.
Histopathological results of the heart (a), liver (b), spleen (c), lung (d), kidney (e) and tumor (f) treated by FA-CDs-DOX group.
Schematic of imaging and targeted therapy of liver cancer using FA-CDs-DOX
Drug loading rates of CD-DOX with different weight ratio of CDs to DOX.

row.entry: Weight of CDs in 1.0 mL solution (mg)
Weight of DOX in 1.0 mL solution (mg)

head.ce:title: Enhanced-fluorescent imaging and targeted therapy of liver cancer using highly luminescent carbon dots-conjugated foliate

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials
Taiyuan University of Technology, Ministry of Education

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have shown great potential in drug delivery and biological imaging applications. In this work, a doxorubicin (DOX) delivery carrier and imaging probe for liver cancer-targeted therapy was designed based on CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (97%), aiming to enhance the antitumor activity and imaging efficiency. Folic acid (FA), which showed high expression in hepatoma cells, was used as targeting components to modify CDs (FA-CDs), and then FA-CDs-DOX was obtained by loading DOX. Results show that CDs and FA-CDs have good biocompatibility, and the DOX release from FA-CDs-DOX is targeted and selective. Confocal microscope demonstrates that FA-CDs-DOX has excellent ability of fluorescence imaging in liver cancer cells. The imaging in vivo shows the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs-DOX is strong enough to penetrate tumor tissue and skin, further verifying its enhanced-fluorescent imaging effects. Tumor inhibition in vivo indicates that the targeting ability of FA-CDs-DOX is significantly higher than that of free DOX, showing obvious better therapeutic effect. To sum up, the targeted and fluorescent drug delivery system based on CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield show an excellent imaging in vivo and tumor inhibition effect, which provide a novel strategy for promoting the potential clinical application of CDs in liver cancer treatment.

Enhanced-fluorescent imaging and targeted therapy of liver cancer using highly luminescent carbon dots-conjugated foliate was presented in this work.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most common malignant tumors, the incidence and mortality of liver cancer is relatively high. Systemic chemotherapy is an important means of treating liver cancer, while conventional chemotherapy drugs have large toxic and side effects during the treatment, which limits drug amount that really enter into the tumor cells. It not only affects the treatment efficiency, but also destroys the immune system, leading to gastrointestinal reactions, myelosuppression and other problems [1,2]. With the development and continuous combination of the nano science with medical technology, targeted nano drug delivery system was explored to achieve the delivery and controlled release of chemotherapy drugs and imaging the process of treatment simultaneously, which is probable to improve the efficiency of chemotherapy for liver cancer [3].

Most of the common nano drug carriers are organic nanoparticles, such as lipid and polymer nanospheres. However, it is very difficult to control their morphology, which makes troubles of delivery and metabolism in vivo [4]. In contrast, inorganic nanoparticles, such as metal nanoparticles, silicon nanoparticles and quantum dots, have become ideal materials for drug delivery due to their unique properties and easy functionalization, which is conducive to the controlled-release delivery of drugs [5–7]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new fluorescent material, overcome the toxicity of heavy metals in traditional semiconductor quantum dots in the biomedical field [8–11]. Due to their good dispersion, biocompatibility, fluorescence, stable optical and chemical properties, and low cost, CDs have a broad application prospect in the fields of drug release and biological imaging.

At present, CDs have been widely studied in the fields of drug delivery and bioimaging [12,13]. To achieve the delivery of chemotherapy drugs, nano drug delivery system based on CDs was constructed by surface modification and coupling chemotherapy drugs [14]. However, these CDs-based nano drug delivery systems are insufficient in their targeting and controlled-release ability, which results in longer delivery time and much leakage of drugs during the delivery process, finally inducing some side effects. Therefore, the effectively targeted drug delivery system based on CDs need to be developed to improve the drug concentration at the targeting site. Moreover, the pH value of liver cancer cell is lower than that of normal cell. This change of liver cancer cell microenvironment can be used as a stimulus to achieve controlled release, ultimately enhancing drug efficacy and reducing adverse effects [15].

In the field of biological imaging, because CDs usually have short emission wavelength, low fluorescence quantum yield and insufficient fluorescence intensity, they cannot penetrate the skin or tissues of organisms. Also considering that the inference of blue light emissive by organisms' self to CDs, it is often difficult to distinguish CDs from biological fluorescence. Therefore, to realize the imaging of CDs in specific parts of the organism becomes an important issue. Strategies were developed to improve the ability of cell penetration of CDs and achieve higher imaging efficiency. CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) were designed or combinated with other materials to enhance their biological imaging ability to monitor tumor effectively due to their outstanding emission fluorescence ability [9].

Herein, in order to heighten the drug releasing and bioimaging of CDs-based nano drug delivery system, this work focused on the research of controlled release system based on CDs with high QY and imaging efficacy (as shown in Scheme 1 ). The used CDs with high QY of 97% were obtained by hydrothermal method with citric acid monohydrate as carbon source and silane coupling agent (KH-792) as additive [16]. The active targeted drug delivery ability was realized by grafting folic acid (FA) on CDs (FA-CDs). Anti-cancer drug DOX was loaded on FA-CDs by the surface group and electrostatic effect to obtain FA-CDs-DOX. The drug release performance of DOX was quantified by flow cytometry. To explore and evaluate the biosafety and tumor inhibition effect of FA-CDs-DOX, in vitro and in vivo experiments were carried out on liver cancer cells and tumor. This study improved the active specific targeting and the fluorescence imaging monitoring ability of CDs-based drug delivery system for liver cancer treatment, achieving the highly efficiency of chemotherapy.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H10O8·H2O) and KH-792 were purchased from Guangfu Science and Technology Development Co. Ltd., Tianjin. DOX, FA, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Phosphate buffer solution, hepatocellular carcinoma cell (HepG2) and hepatocyte (HL-7702) were provided by Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Wuhan. Human embryonic kidney cell (293T) and cervical cancer cell (HeLa) were donated by the Transformation Center Laboratory of Shanxi Medical University. Disposable membrane (0.22 μm) came from Lianghe Glass Instrument Company, Taixing. The dialysis bag (1000 Da) was obtained from Yuansong Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Other medical devices were made by Solarbio Reagent Company, Beijing. Super pure water (H2O) was made by our own laboratory.

Electrothermal dryer (GZX-9140MBE) was provided by Boxun Industrial Co., Ltd., Shanghai. High-pressure hydrothermal reactor was made by Zhize Biotechnology Development Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Pipette (WKYIV-250) was purchased from Refining Biochemical Reagent Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Ultrasound cleaner (KQ-300DE) was provided by Kunshan Ultrasound Instrument Co., Ltd. Dark-box tripurpose ultraviolet analyzer (ZF-20D) was supplied by Lihui Scientific Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai. Enzyme scaler (Thermo Fisher Scientif) was provided by Finland.

Morphology of products was analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-100CXII, Japan). The particle size and zeta potential of the products were measured by dynamic light scattering (Nano-zs90, Malvern, UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, TENSOR 27, Germany BRUKER), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI5000 Versa Probe type, Japanese ULVAC-PHI) and elemental analyzer (EA, Vario EL/micro cube type, German EA) were used to analyze the surface functional groups, chemical bonds and element content of the product. Flow cytometer were used to detect the viability of cells (FACS-Calibur, BD, USA). Cellular images were acquired with inverted microscope (CLSM Olympus FV100, Japan). Small animal vivo imaging instrument (Xtreme In-Vivo, Bruker, USA) was used to measure the state of tumor on the mice.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method as previously reported [16]. In the specific experiment process, 1.05 g of C6H10O8·H2O, 21.80 mL of KH-792 and 40 mL of deionized water were added to 100 mL PTFE stainless steel autoclave step by step, in which the molar ratio of C6H10O8·H2O to KH-792 was 1:20. The mixture was heated to 200 °C keeping for 6 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. It can be seen that the solution changed from colourless to orange. The reaction mixture was then filtered with a disposable membrane to remove some unreacted impurities. Subsequently, CDs were purified by dialysis bag (MW: 1000 Da) for 3 h and dialyzed for several times. Finally, the CDs powder was obtained by lyophilization.

Subsequently, 8 mg of FA, 4 mg of CDs, 3 mg of EDC and 3 mg of NHS were added into 10 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4) and stirred for 24 h at 25 °C. FA can react with γ-carboxylic acid and the amino group on the surface of the CDs by an amide reaction. After the reaction, the supernatant was taken out and filtered by a membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was then encapsulated in a 1000 Da dialysis bag in 2 L ultra-pure water, dialyzing for 24 h. FA-CDs powder was obtained by lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FA-CDs-DOX by loading DOX
ce:section.ce:para: DOX has been widely used in the treatment of various cancers, including cervical cancer, gastric cancer, lung cancer and liver cancer, because it can directly enter the nuclear DNA to kill cancer cells. Therefore, it was used as a model drug to evaluate the therapeutic efficiency of different carriers in this work. To optimize the DOX loading conditions, DOX amount on CDs was firstly investigated. Three different drug-loading ratios (total mass 10 mg, mCDs:mDOX = 4:1, 1:1 and 1:4) were allocated, and 20 mL of ultrapure water was put into a shaking table to avoid light at 25 °C for 24 h, promoting the loading of DOX on CDs. After loading, the mixture was packaged in 1000 Da dialysis bag in the dark environment, and then put into 2 L of ultrapure water with stirring continuously for 48 h. Fresh ultrapure water was replaced every 24 h to remove the unloaded drugs. The DOX loading system was labeled as CDs-DOX. The DOX loading on FA-CDs were conducted under the same condition, labeled as FA-CDs-DOX.

The loading ratio of the drug carrier was calculated by mDOX/mCDs×100%, in which mDOX and mCDs were calculated by the standard curve of CDs and DOX, respectively [8]. The calculation methods are as follows: firstly, the standard curves of CDs and DOX were obtained at 358 and 488 nm, separately. Then, the absorbance of CDs-DOX was measured, and their mass was calculated according to the standard curves. At last, the loading ratio was obtained. After the experiment, CDs-DOX with the highest drug loading capacity was selected for subsequent experiments.

The static release method was used to examine the release performance of CDs-DOX at different pH. Firstly, the purified CDs-DOX was dissolved in 50 mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Then, 10 mL of CDs-DOX was put into dialysis bag (1000 Da) and immersed them in 100 mL of PBS in a constant temperature shaker with a light-proof. Every 4 h, 2 mL of dialysate was analyzed by UV–Vis (at 488 nm), and the concentration of DOX in the release solution was calculated. The DOX release rate R = mi/m0 × 100%, in which R is drug release rate, mi and m0 are the mass of DOX released into PBS and the mass of initial DOX carried by CDs, separately. Fresh PBS was supplemented into the dialysis bag. The release of CDs-DOX at pH 5.5 was similarly measured.

In addition, to observe the intracellular release of CDs-DOX, 2 mL (0.625 μg/mL) of CDs-DOX was incubated with HepG2 cells for 0.5, 4 and 8 h, on which a series of confocal laser images were obtained. The distribution of CDs and DOX in cancer cells at different time was determined and the sustained release property was observed by CLSM. In this experiment, the excitation lights were set at the wavelength of 405 and 488 nm. The blue light (405 nm) was used to excite CDs, and the red light (488 nm) was used to excite DOX. The data were collected and analyzed by F10-ASM 1.7 Viewer software. Flow cytometry was used to quantitatively analyze the sustained release of DOX.

Four kinds of cells (HepG2, HL-7702, 293T and Hela) were selected and incubated with different concentrations of CDs (400, 300, 200, 150, 100, 75, 50 and 37.5 μg/mL) for 24 and 48 h, respectively. CCK8 method was used to detect the toxicity of CDs in each cell.

The effect of CDs on apoptosis was further studied by flow cytometry. Taking 7702 cells as blank control, and then the cells were incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL) for 12 and 24 h, separately. The apoptosis was analyzed by FACS Calibur analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeting imaging in vitro and in vivo

ce:section.ce:para: To explore the recognition ability for folate receptor positive cells of FA-CDs, HepG2 cells were incubated in confocal culture dish for 0.5 h at 37 °C with 2 mL of CDs and FA-CDs at the concentration of 200 μg/mL, respectively. Laser confocal microscopy was used to observe the distribution of nanoparticles in cancer cells and to explore their performance of imaging. Taking 405 nm as excitation wavelength, the luminescence intensity was used to evaluate the ability of imaging. The high fluorescence intensity observed can be attributed to the entrance of drug carriers into the cells.

In order to further determine the targeting effect, the distribution of CDs and DOX in the cells was also detected. 2 mL of FA-CDs-DOX (0.3125 μg/mL) and HepG2 cells was incubated for 24 h, and then the intracellular distribution of the complex was observed by CLSM.

5-week-old BALB/c nude mice were housed according to the requirements for experimental animals, facilities and environment (GB14925-2010). They were breed under a condition of 12 h light/dark cycle, controlled temperature (20–26 °C), controlled humidity (40–70%) and free access to water and food without specific pathogens. The average weight of all mice is about 16 g. The approval number of the investigation on nude mice is SYDL2019008.

All operations were performed under anesthesia to minimize pain. 50 μL of CDs (200 μg/mL), DOX, FA-CDs-DOX (20 μg/mL) were injected into the tumor to observe the tumor's fluorescence imaging, separately. The optical images were recorded by small animal live imager (λex = 480 nm, λem = 600 nm) and analyzed by Bruker MI SE system.

MTT assay was used to compare the inhibition of DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX under different concentrations (10, 5, 2.5, 1.25, 0.625, 0.3125, 0.1563 and 0.078 μg/mL) on HL-7702 and HepG2 cells, respectively. Because the loading capacity of DOX on CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX was different, their concentration was calculated with the same concentration of DOX. HL-7702 and HepG2 cells were incubated with DOX for 24 and 48 h, separately.

To establish a model of hepatic malignant tumors, HepG2 cells in logarithmic growth phase were taken out and washed with PBS twice. The cells were digested with 0.25% trypsin-EDTA solution, and the digestion was terminated by adding serum-containing medium. The supernatant was removed after centrifugation at 800 rpm. Subsequently, the cells were re-suspended with serum-free medium and the concentration was adjusted to 8 × 107/mL. 0.1 mL of cell suspension was injected into BALB/c nude mice by 27-gauge needle on upper part of the right trunk (i.e. below the shoulder armor). The formation of tumor was observed twice a week.

When the size of tumors reached about 200 mm3, 15 nude mice were randomly divided into five groups, and treated by PBS (200 μL, pH = 7.4), CDs (200 μg/mL, 100 μL), DOX (20 μg/mL, 100 μL), CDs-DOX(20 μg/mL, 100 μL) and FA-CDs-DOX (20 μg/mL, 100 μL) through caudal vein, respectively. The first group mice were injected PBS solution as control group. The dosage of the injection was determined by the weight of the mice and the size of the tumors, and controlled within the tolerance range of the mice. All injections were repeated 4 times at every 5 days. The size of the tumors and the weight of the mice were observed regularly. The long diameter a (mm) and short diameter b (mm) of the tumors were measured, respectively. The volume of the tumors was calculated by using the equation: V = 0.5 ab2 mm3. The tumor inhibition rate (Ti) was calculated as follows:
T i
=
T v
−
T 0
/
T 0
× 100 %
in which Tv was the volume of tumors before regularly injection (drugs, PBS) and T0 was the initial volume of tumors [8]. All the infection, reduction of food intake or impaired activity were observed until the mice were executed.

The mice were euthanized after treatment for 20 days, and their hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, kidneys and tumors were taken out for observation. The group treated by PBS was used as blank control. The organ indices were recorded and analyzed by using small animal living imager (λex = 480 nm, λem = 600 nm). Real time image 3.1 software (Bruker MI SE) was used to collect and analyze the images. Then the tissues were embedded in wax blocks and prepared into slice by 4 mm. HE staining was performed to observe the tissue condition.

Statistical results are expressed as mean standard deviation. Test cytotoxicity test were compared with the ANOVA and the Student-Newman-Keuls tests, and repeated measurement variance analysis was used in tumor volume change. In this work, when the P < 0.05, the definition of numerical difference is significant; P < 0.001, the definition of numerical difference is extremely significant.

DOX loading on the CDs can be attributed to electrostatic interaction, π-π accumulation, Van der Waals force and hydrophobic bonds [7–9]. As shown in Fig. 1(a, b), CDs show bright blue emitting with a size of about 2.7 nm and good water solubility. Moreover, abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs are beneficial for the DOX loading (as show FITR, XPS and element analysis in Figs. S1, S2 and Table S1 in the Supplementary material). In order to verify whether DOX could be loaded on the CDs, Zeta potentials of different products' PBS solution were measured (Fig. 1c). It can be seen that CDs are negatively charged (−3.07 mV) in the PBS solution, while DOX' PBS solution is positive charged (+19.47 mV), which is beneficial to the drug loading owing to the mutually attractive electrostatic force. After grafting of negative charged FA, the surface charge of FA-CDs (−12.80 mV) is further reduced, which is more conducive to drug loading. The loading of DOX can be confirmed by the positive charged of CDs-DOX (+7.48 mV) and FA-CDs-DOX (+3.39 mV).

The drug loading process was further explored by UV–Vis absorption of different products. In Fig. 2a, the absorption of CDs-DOX show both the absorption peaks of CDs (358 nm) and DOX (488 nm), which indicates that DOX is loaded on the surface of CDs. Similarly, the UV–Vis absorption curves of FA-CDs and FA-CDs-DOX (Fig. 2b) also reflect the loading of FA and DOX. In order to obtain FA-CDs-DOX with high DOX loading capacity, the effect of different ratios between CDs and DOX on the drug loading capacity was investigated. The drug loading rates were calculated by the standard curves, as shown in Fig. 2c and d. As for CDs and DOX, their standard curves are y = 1.47735x and y = 17.9836x, respectively.

Table 1 lists the DOX loading rate under different conditions. When the weight ratio of CDs to DOX is 1:4, 1:1 and 4:1, the loading rate of DOX are 13.07%, 31.43% and 16.91%, respectively. It illustrates that the highest loading capability could be obtained when CDs and DOX mixed at the same quality. DOX can be loaded on CDs through several interactions, including hydrogen bond, electrostatic interaction, interaction of conjugated ring, chemical interactions between the amino groups and carboxyl groups, the force generated by amine group of DOX and hydroxyl group of CDs, and so on [17]. The highest loading rate of DOX was obtained at the weight mass ration of 1:1. Therefore, CDs-DOX with the weight ratio of CDs to DOX of 1:1 was used in subsequent experiments.

The controlled release performance of CDs-DOX was studied by encapsulated them in dialysis bag and placed in PBS solution. It is reported that the release of DOX in nano drug carrier system could be affected by pH [18,19]. Therefore, the intracellular lysosomal acid conditions and normal cell neutral conditions were simulated to study the in vitro release kinetics of CDs-DOX in pH 5.5 and 7.4. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . At pH = 5.5, DOX is released about 79.31% at 24 h and 84.88% at 48 h, while at pH = 7.4, DOX is only released about 14.13% at 24 h and 16.39% at 48 h, respectively, demonstrating that DOX is released faster in acid environment than in neutral environment. The slow release of DOX in neutral environment can minimize the extracellular loss of drug molecules before entering the tumor cells and avoid damaging to normal cells. However, when drug delivery systems are phagocytized by tumor cells with low pH, most DOX can be released continuously and effectively, thus enhancing the therapeutic effect of cancer. This pH responsive release can be attributed to two reasons. First, in acid environment, the protonation of the amino groups in DOX is increased, and DOX becomes hydrophilic, leading to a higher releasing rate [20,21]. Second, the hydrazone bonds connecting DOX and CDs are unstable in acid condition [8]. Because both the extracellular tissue of tumor cells and the microenvironment of intracellular lysosomes and ribosomes are acidic, pH responsive can control the release of DOX only in cancer cells rather than normal cells, and finally improve the release rate and reduce the side effects of drugs.
To observe the intracellular release of DOX in vitro, a group of confocal laser images were obtained by incubating CDs-DOX with HepG2 cells for 0.5 h (Fig. 4a–d), 4 h (Fig. 4e–h) and 8 h (Fig. 4i–l).

When the cells are excited at 405 nm for different time, they emit blue light from CDs, while excited at 488 nm, red light from DOX is observed. With the extension of time, it can be seen that blue and red fluorescence gradually increased, which indicates that the amount of CDs and DOX in HepG2 cells is increasing, and gradually enters the nucleus, verifying that CDs-DOX has an intracellular drug release effect.

In order to quantify the release of DOX and further verify the controlled release effect of CDs-DOX, the time gradient curves of DOX release were measured by incubating CDs-DOX and DOX with HepG2 cell at 0, 0.5, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 and 24 h (as shown in Fig. 5a and b), separately. The emission intensities of DOX in different time were recorded under the excited wavelength of 550 nm, and DOX release amount were quantified with the mean emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 5a and d, the DOX release for CDs-DOX-HepG2 group increases continuously with an average fluorescence intensity of 820.86 at 24 h. While with the increase of time, the DOX amount in the tumor cells for DOX-HepG2 group reaches a peak value with an average fluorescence intensity of 378.76 at 0.5 h, and then the fluorescence intensity initially decreases, which is followed by a slow increase with an average fluorescence intensity of 211.40 at 24 h (Fig. 5b and d). The results show that the total amount of DOX released from CDs-DOX-HepG2 group is significantly higher than that from DOX-HepG2 group, which further demonstrates that CDs-DOX has obvious drug-controlled release effect and is suitable for the drug delivery.

The release of CDs-DOX in 7702 cells (named as CDs-DOX-7702) under the same condition were also conducted to reveal DOX release difference between cancer cells and normal cells. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the DOX release amount from CDs-DOX-7702 group slightly increases, and then reaches an equilibrium at 16 h. Finally, the average fluorescence intensity at 24 h is 180.95, which is significantly lower than that of in HepG2 cells (820.86). These results further prove that CDs-DOX has preferable release property for cancer cells with acid environment, according with DOX release results in different pH that has been studied in Section 3.2.1.

Generally speaking, the toxicity of biomedical nanomaterials affects the activity of cells and living organisms, and even leads to the apoptosis of cells and living organisms. Therefore, low toxicity and good biocompatibility are the necessary conditions for nanomaterials to be used in organism [22]. The biocompatibility of CDs was investigated before they were used in vivo.

In this work, two tumor cells (HepG2 and HeLa) and two normal cells (7702 and 293 T) were selected to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , the toxicity of CDs is quite low. After incubation with 400 μg/mL of CDs for 24 h, the viability of four kinds of cells is maintained above 86%, and after incubation for 48 h, the viability is also still maintained above 70%. The viability of the four kinds of cells is similar with no significant statistical difference (P > 0.05). The result proves that the CDs are safe for different cells, and have excellent biocompatibility.

The apoptosis of 7702 cells affected by CDs was recorded by flow cytometry [23,24], as shown in Fig. 7 . After incubation with CDs for 24 h, the early apoptosis is 3.18%, and the late apoptosis is 1.76%. After 48 h, the early apoptosis is 5.37%, and the late apoptosis is 2.17%. These results are similar to those of the blank control experiment without CDs, which shows that the influence of CDs on the early and late apoptosis of 7702 cells is unobvious. It is also consistent with the previous observation of 7702 cell viability in Fig. 6. In conclusion, CDs have excellent biocompatibility and can be used as an ideal carrier for targeted anti-tumor drug delivery and treatment.

Combined with biomolecular ligands, fluorescent nanoparticles often show various targeted therapy and multifunction properties [23,25,26]. Therefore, they have been widely used in in vitro and in vivo cell imaging, providing an important way to target cancer cells [27–29]. The targeting imaging performance of CDs and FA-CDs in vitro were measured to explore the recognition ability of folate antibody. 200 μg/mL of CDs and FA-CDs were incubated with HepG2 cells for 0.5 h, separately, and then the confocal laser scanning microscopy images were given in Fig. 8 . Compared with Fig. 8c and f, obvious bright blue fluorescence in the cells can be observed after imaged by FA-CDs, while little blue fluorescence is detected after imaged by CDs. It illustrates that FA-CDs can target into the cancer cells due to the high expression of FA receptor on the surface of HepG2, demonstrating their great potential in fluorescent probe. In addition, the morphology of the cells that incubated with CDs and FA-CDs is still normal, which indicates that CDs can be used as a safe fluorescent probe.

Once entering the cells, most of the nanomaterials are uptaken by lysosomes, while DOX can enter into the tumor nucleus and destroys the DNA, leading to the apoptosis of cells. In order to determine the location of CDs and DOX that released from FA-CDs-DOX in the cells, the fluorescence imaging study was carried out after incubation FA-CDs-DOX with HepG2 cells for 24 h, as shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that CDs appear in the cytoplasm (Fig. 9b, blue emission), and DOX enters into the nucleus (Fig. 9c, red emission). Therefore, the drug-loaded complex FA-CDs-DOX can be used as a good fluorescent agent to tracker the delivery of DOX.

In order to further explore the potential fluorescence performance of nano DOX-loaded complex, the imaging properties of CDs, free DOX and FA-CDs-DOX in vivo were investigated. Fig. 10a shows the photographs of the CDs, DOX and FA-CDs-DOX suspension excited by 480 nm light. They emit blue, yellow-green and orange-red, respectively, which demonstrates that the fluorescence intensities of FA-CDs-DOX are much stronger than that of CDs due to the introduction of DOX with red emission. Then, the suspension of CDs, free DOX and FA-CDs-DOX were injected into three tumor-beared mice, respectively. The results show that due to the influence of biological self-fluorescence, both CDs and DOX fail to penetrate the tumor tissue, while FA-CDs-DOX can still clearly reflect the outline of tumor (Fig. 10b). Meanwhile, after penetrating the tumor tissue and skin, the fluorescence intensity in vivo decreases a lot. It is found that no hair loss, nausea, vomiting, and other symptoms appear in three mice within 24 h. The good biocompatibility and fluorescence imaging properties of FA-CDs-DOX verify their great potential in the future biological tumor imaging and biomedical applications.

Targeting tumor inhibition of nano-carriers in vitro was evaluated on HepG2 cells [30,31]. Fig. 11a and b compare the inhibition effects of DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX on HepG2 cells for 24 and 48 h. With the increase of DOX concentration, the cell viability shows a significant downward trend, proving the high efficacy of DOX. It can be seen that when the concentration of DOX and drug-loaded complex is 10 μg/mL, more than 40% of the cells die after 24 h. Further increase of cell death is observed when the incubation time extends to 48 h. The inhibition of CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX also results in dose-dependent death of HepG2 cells. CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX present the similar trend, the cell survival rates decrease with the increase of the drug concentrations. Especially, compared with free DOX, the inhibition of FA-CDs-DOX is obvious, and shows much higher inhibition both in 24 and 48 h. There is significant statistical difference (P < 0.05).

To investigate different inhibition effects of the drug-loading complex on the normal cells and cancer cells, DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX were incubated with 7702 cells for 24 and 48 h, respectively. The results show that FA-CDs-DOX exhibits no significant toxicity to normal hepatocytes. After 24 h of incubation, 10 μg/mL of FA-CDs-DOX is enough to kill 90% of HepG2 cells, while the viability of 7702 cells is still over 80%. This can be attributed to three aspects: (1) the difference of pH value between the normal cell and cancer cell lead to DOX has selective release in cancer cells [15]; (2) because nanoparticles can be absorbed by enhanced permeability and retention effect of tumor tissue, CDs can improve the delivery efficiency of DOX in tumor cells [7,8]; (3) combining with the interactions with over-expressed FA receptor on HepG2 cells, FA endows the targeting of FA-CDs-DOX on tumor cells, leading the controlled and oriented release of DOX [5]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the side effects of CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX are less than those of free DOX, and the therapeutic effect of FA-CDs-DOX grafted with FA is better than that of CDs-DOX. These results are very helpful to reduce drug dosage and optimize treatment effect. Meanwhile, the side effects of DOX, such as cardiac toxicity, liver function damage, gastrointestinal reaction, and skin reaction, can be significantly improved.

The targeting tumor inhibition in vivo experiment was conducted on the tumor-beared mice. The size of the tumor was recorded, as shown in Fig. 12a. After treating for 20 days, the Ti of DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX are 41.95%, 66.02% and 72.13%, respectively. The growth of tumor treated with CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX is 24.07% and 30.18% slower than that of DOX group. Fig. 12d shows the photographs of nude mice treated with different drugs before they were executed. It can be seen that the treatment of FA-CDs-DOX on the tumor is the most effective. Finally, the tumor was taken out, and their sizes were shown in Fig. 12b and c. From bottom to up, they were treated by PBS control group, CDs, DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX, respectively. The sizes of tumors treated by DOX and their drug complex are obvious smaller that of control group.

Compared with PBS control group and CDs group, all DOX, CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX group showed inhibition on tumor growth, and the inhibition rate has a statistical difference (χ2(Tv10) = 10.113, P = 0.002; χ2(Tv15) = 47.559, P < 0.001; χ2(Tv20) = 16.593, P < 0.001) after treating tumor for 10 days, which was discussed in the Supplementary material (Tables S2 and S3). These results indicate that the targeting abilities of CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX are significantly higher than that of free DOX, showing obvious better therapeutic effect. This can be attributed to the accumulation of CDs-DOX and FA-CDs-DOX in tumor, and the sustained release of DOX. Moreover, there is no side effect after multiple injections of FA-CDs-DOX, due to its active targeting, passive targeting and excellent drug release performance. Therefore, FA-CDs-DOX will be an effective nano drug carrier system for tumor treatment.

In order to verify the targeting of FA-CDs-DOX in vivo, the distribution of free DOX and FA-CDs-DOX in various living tissues was observed. After euthanasia, the main organs of mice were collected for imaging in small animal in vivo imager, as shown in Fig. 13 . Fig. 13a shows the imaging of the organs treated by free DOX group. The imaging of heart, liver, kidney and a small part of tumor tissue may be related to the cardiotoxicity and hepatotoxicity of DOX [30,31]. Interestingly, in the treatment of FA-CDs-DOX group (Fig. 13b), most of the fluorescence was detected in tumor tissue, and only slight fluorescence was found in liver and kidney, whose intensity is quite lower than that of free DOX group. It is suggested that FA-CDs-DOX may be excreted by liver and kidney, and multiple injections do not damage to heart and liver, which greatly reduces the side effects of DOX. These results confirm that FA-CDs-DOX present unique tumor targeting effects, which is relate to high EPR effect and active targeting of FA-CDs-DOX.

To further explore the safety of nano drug complex, after the imaging test, the tumor, heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of mice were immediately embedded in wax blocks, and then sectioned and stained with hematoxylin eosin (H&E) 1, as shown in Fig. 14 . Histopathological results show that normal organs (Fig. 14a–e) show unobvious changes and tumor (Fig. 14f) has extensive necrosis, confirming that FA-CDs-DOX is a good nano carrier with low toxicity. Histopathological results for other groups were shown in Fig. S3 in the Supplementary material.

Moreover, all the mice survived for 8 weeks without any symptoms, such as violence, drowsiness, hair loss, vomiting, dysentery or death, which are similar to those of the control group (PBS). These results indicate that FA-CDs-DOX has low toxicities both in vivo and in vitro.

In summary, in order to effectively improve liver cancer therapy and monitoring, this study presented a novel targeting DOX delivery system and imaging probe based on CDs with high QY. The release of DOX from CDs exhibits a faster rate in acidic than neutral environments, which is conducive to target of tumor cells. CLSM images verify that CDs-DOX indeed has a targeting release effect in HepG2 cells. Low toxicity and good compatibility of FA-CDs-DOX are confirmed by cell viability test. In vivo imaging, bright fluorescence clearly reflects the outline of the tumor, which further indicates FA-CDs-DOX has excellent targeting and imaging properties. The growth of liver cancer beared on mice is more efficiently inhibited by FA-CDs-DOX than free DOX along with the decrease of side effects of DOX. Meanwhile, the treatment is monitored by the fluorescence imaging. In general, CDs with high QY can be used as an ideal drug carrier and imaging probe in liver cancer treatment. This research lays a good foundation for the integration of tumor targeted treatment and imaging monitoring.

Shicai Wang: Data curation. Lin Chen: Writing - original draft. Junli Wang: Methodology. Jinglei Du: Formal analysis. Qiang Li: Investigation. Yuduan Gao: Conceptualization. Shiping Yu: Supervision. Yongzhen Yang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shanxi Provincial Excellent Talents Science and Technology Innovation Project (201805D211001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803148, U1710117), and Graduate Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2019SY282), and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201901D211502 and 201901D211501).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111233.
